sentences.text: So now through our work with the roll wagon, we could visit all camps.
A normal prisoner had no chance whatsoever.
He wouldn't even know what's going on.
Because most of them were in such terrible condition, hungry, beaten, tired from work.
Going to work, going back to work, and waiting for their death, or getting out of the camps.
I remember once a little boy of us, Pauli was his name.
A German boy was caught.
And he got-- he was punished.
And as a punishment, he had to be in the box of where they hang people.
There was the gallows, and the box.
And the box was working when they put a kind of folding door.
And they put him into this box with the folding door.
And poor chap, he cried the whole night.
But nothing worse happened to him.
Yeah, after.
Because he was a very small.
He was about 12 years, and looking very small so.
In our yard, in this number 13, there was the gallows, and also the table where they punished people.
There were some people who came there for punishment, to get-- I don't know-- 30 or 50 times to be hit.
And he had to count it.
And there was a whole procedure.
And sometimes we had to see it.
And sometimes we had to go into the block.
But we could, of course, see through the wooden-- the openings.
And I remember once somebody ran away.
And there were many people who tried--
Of course.
They were caught, and they had to have the kind of [NON-ENGLISH] They caught me.
And they were terribly punished.
I remember one SS man took this huge stick, which the barrel of soup, where people were carrying with two sticks of wood, the metal had some hooks.
And he took this one stick, and was running after this poor man.
And they beat him so severe that the stick broke.
And he was lumps of flesh.
It was horrible.
And everyone-- most probably.
I couldn't see him dying before my eyes.
But he was in a terrible shape.
And we had to witness it.
On this yard, there was selection.
And this yard we saw when a Russian POW, this prisoner of war came, with a special group.
And then they sent him somewhere.
Most probably they killed him.
They were very strong men.
And--
[INAUDIBLE] at, children they played, and made some jokes.
I Remember an engineer from Leningrad, and the [INAUDIBLE] or something.
Because he took our fingers and, it's like a joke.
Hit us, but his fingers were like this.
Anyhow, they played with us.
But I also remember [INAUDIBLE] who tried to escape.
They caught him.
Yeah, a Russian.
And two or three, they caught.
But this [INAUDIBLE], he-- and somebody else, cut his wrist.
And that was the early afternoon.
Because we were during the whole day--
Yeah, with the roll wagon, we were more or less there.
And the executions were in the afternoon.
Before the camp, I mean the entrance of the camp, where the kitchen was.
And there was a little music.
The orchestra, they had to play.
And the whole camp came forward.
And we had to witness the execution.
But this man who tried to kill himself to cut his wrist, this SS man called the first aid from Auschwitz in a special order.
And he said, this man has to live, I think, until 4 o'clock or 6 o'clock, when the appel was, and when he had to be hanged.
So he had to be kept alive to put back to life, and to be kept alive.
So we should be hanged in their presence.
Three people were hanged at the same time.
And there was music.
And we had to look.
And I remember the Russian soldier, whatever, even said just before hanging, Stalin or something nationalistic.
And I remember on another execution where they put some wires on this man.
This was a Jewish man who tried to escape.
And of course before he was severely beaten.
And then they gave-- wanted to give him a last cigarette.
But he spit it out.
This execution we had to witness.
A couple of hours after they were dead, they took them down, and carried them away.
Well, that was in our block, this equipment, this punishment with the stick took place in the block.
And the gallows was sometimes taken to another place.
We could, as I told you, had access to this Sonderkommando.
And here there was something extraordinary.
They had great pity on us.
Because there were no children in the camps at that time.
Later on came some other children from Hungaria.
Only with the-- yeah.
In the gas chambers.
And did all kinds of jobs.
And I asked the men I knew very well, this was this [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE] Zuckerman.
And the other one was Kalman Furman.
And I remember his number 80,810.
And from [?
Luna ?] near Grodno.
After the appel, I could sneak in and they gave us food.
I remember even one was-- that they called him the dentist.
Their job was to take out the golden tooth.
And they smuggled in gold.
Well, they knew they were-- I know exactly when they were executed.
See, and I asked--
Of course.
Yeah, yeah.
They were-- they always changed and they would say, uprising in October '44 in the crematorium.
It wasn't successful.
But a few people survived, which were staying.
And all which were trying to run away were shot, killed, or caught and killed.
No.
No, nobody as far as I-- from Auschwitz running away, yes.
There were a few prisoners who run away.
Even there are famous cases of two prisoners who later on gave a report to--
Even to the Pope and so on.
But it was difficult to believe what's going on.
Vrba, I think, was one name.
And anyhow, so these people come in, and told me exactly what was his job.
He told me the first job was he had to burn his own parents.
I mean they took them away, and the first time they opened the gas chamber, it was his parents, his transport.
And he didn't want to do it.
So he wanted to hang himself.
But the safe team had to give him a different job.
He came to be a kind of a sewing with the sewing machine to do [INAUDIBLE] repair.
But his job was holding people at single executions, when they were shooting them.
And he told me, when people were full of unrest or so, he had to hold them on the ear, that they shouldn't move too strongly.
And the SS man shoots him here in the neck.
If they were not willing to go calmly.
And later on I asked him about all the details.
He said, ah, why do you need to know it.
Nobody will anyhow come out of here.
But I talked to him in a kind of children's-- look.
I am young.
And you never know.
And maybe I could come out.
And then I will tell you-- then I will tell people about you, a kind of promise I gave him.
And these people slowly, two or three of them told me about their work in detail.
And I was curious.
I wanted to know everything.
How it goes on, and what happens when a transport comes.
And let's say I talked to him about Edelstein.
And he said, oh, don't believe on this unit master.
They are all traitors.
But I said, no.
I know Edelstein very well from Theresienstadt.
He was a wonderful man.
I remember he went up from his meal, helping somebody.
A woman came in and said, oh my husband is in the transport.
Please take him out.
And we went out, and tried to help her.
And in his flat, everyone could go in.
He wasn't like one of them.
And apropos, later on, I met somebody who was in the bunker in Auschwitz Eins with Edelstein.
And he told me he behaved like a saint.
You're talking about Rumkowski.
Yeah.
What is that?
Oh, the cat.
Ah, the cat.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Why?
Because in [INAUDIBLE] they're treated as an individual as I told you.
Like Rumkowski when we came that they made the whole fuss with Rumkowski.
They took him on the side.
He had to witness his whole transport.
He had to go and see how the whole transport goes to the [BACKGROUND NOISE] gas, and so-and-so-and-so.
And in the end, he was treated like a partisan.
I mean he had to say [NON-ENGLISH] before the oven, he was shot and fell in the oven.
That's was also how they treated--
In the time when Lodz was liquidated.
Yes.
I was there.
And I remember it very well.
And I will tell you about it.
But now I wanted to tell about Kalman.
He told me all the details.
Kalman Furman.
Sorry?
No, he didn't.
He was 24 or 25 at that time.
And I only met one or two Sonderkommando after the war, but whom I didn't know at that time.
He taught me how it was.
That if a transport came, and they didn't know what's going on.
So the SS man, and now I speak about the crematorium 1 and 2.
Hurry up.
And the soup will getting cold.
And please remember the number of your hook.
There were some benches around--
And remember to put shoes nicely together.
You should find everything.
And if everyone was naked, they pushed them further on in the gas chamber.
And when everyone was in, they closed it with a kind of hook.
And then they poured, this SS man poured Zyklon B from above, in the crematorium 1 and 2.
There were two huge gas chambers.
In each gas chambers there were two kind of holes, like lift, but 40 centimeters and 40 centimeters.
They had iron like chicken-- you know, how they call chicken.
Yeah, but very heavy, and kind of, you could see through.
Yeah.
Four bars and around kinds of fence, iron fence, chicken fence as we call it.
But very heavy.
And when you went in, in the chamber, as long as it light, there was some light.
And there was some installation of a shower.
But that was very curious.
And I went near to it.
And the body of the shower had-- hadn't even a real hose.
It was only--
As if-- so there was no water at all.
Underneath there were kind of installation for ventilation, and for later on for washing with water.
Because it was very dirty he told me.
And then I saw, I mean the people could-- and from above, the SS man took up a kind of cover, a square cover, like a canalization you have sometimes.
A square cover, you put it up, with a gas mask here, and throw in the Zyklon B.
It evaporated rather quickly.
It depends on the temperature.
And as far as they told me, people were after-- from five minutes until 20 minutes, 30 minutes dead.
But before that there were terrible scenes.
They could see it through a peephole.
And everyone tried climb up.
Some mother and children-- were pressing the children.
And sometimes it even happened, there were a few cases where children survived.
And the Sonderkommando tried to help them, but they couldn't.
The SS shoots them.
There was such cases that they left these poor people, leave for a couple of hours.
There were cases, once a transport came, there was a dancer.
And they told me the name was Schelling or something case, SS men.
And she was dancing when she was half naked or completely naked.
And she managed to take his pistol and shoot him.
And there was a kind of terribly blood bath.
He told me they had to work until very late in-- full of blood, until they cleaned it.
Because that happened not in the gas chambers, but before the gas chambers.
But they had to clean it.
When she tried to--
Yeah.
This one SS she killed, Schellinger or so was his name.
Of course they killed her afterward.
They killed many people.
Yes.
She killed him.
She was dancing a different, and somehow around, and-- that he told me.
Then he told me many stories about how people behaved.
Sometimes transfer of Jews from ghettos, and how the mentality of people is that everyone wants to live another minute, and is willing to sacrifice just to live another minute.
So sometimes there came very strong men, and the Sonderkommando tried to encourage them that they should make some resistance.
But the minute the Germans saw it, they choose one, the women or some of the elderly, and told them if you will calm them and so on, we will let you live or something like that.
But of course they never have done it.
So all kinds of not so pleasant stories he told me of how people behaved.
If they didn't let them easily in, there was a special place with only sticks.
It was the second time I saw these famous sticks, a little chamber with only sticks.
And I asked him, what is this, when I was led in the crematorium.
He said, well, that's for the stick commander.
If they don't want to go we have to--
Yeah, yeah.
And there I also saw--
Well, they could kill as far as they told me, a whole transport overnight, 2,500 people in one crematorium.
It depends if he had to wait in the queue--
Very smooth?
So--
I mean he was in the gas chamber, he was around 20 minutes--
--half an hour, as far as they told me.
And then only they took the corpse out.
Yeah.
And then later I saw the places, exactly.
Other children were not so curious.
But other children with me went later on, with me--
-- with the roll wagon, and we went to the crematorium.
And the one, sometimes it happens it was very cold, and we finished our job, you could go in only of course, in the compound when there was no transport.
Our job was to take wood.
There was tremendous huge blocks of wood, which were used in the time of the Hungarian Jews, when the capacity of the four crematorium were not enough.
And they built a new huge pit.
And this pit was done.
In this way they put a lot of this wood, then benzene, and then the corpse.
And all this tremendous wood was laying in the crematorium, and around the crematorium.
And we brought the wood for normal heating in the autumn of Birkenau.
And if we loaded our roll wagon, everyone would sit and the kapo would say, children you can warm yourself in the gas chambers.

And because I knew all of it from before from the stories, I wanted to see everything, where is the special kind of place where it just was sliding old people and old cripples in the end of the end [NON-ENGLISH] around, which was where the people take off their clothes, was a shift from above down.
I imagine.
But then, of course, they went also to the gas chambers.
Then I saw the lift.
There was a lift of about a measurement 2 meters and 1 meter, which was only for the bodies, after the gassing, they took them out.
And with the lift, they put them up where really the ovens were.
And how well I know this people and crematorium there's a little story.
I had an uncle still, this [INAUDIBLE] from Bruna who was in Theresienstadt.
And once, one of the members of the Sonderkommando, I think it was through Zuckerman, I think so, his job was that night to use one of the ovens only for documents.
There was also one oven where they burned the documents,
No, no.
Only the documents, the passports, and I don't know what.
And he told me if they could, of course, and there was some value that some rich people had some dollars or other monies.
And so they couldn't take it out or save it somewhere.
So they burned it.
No.
They couldn't take it out.
It was terribly dangerous.
And they told me what they have done with people they caught.
They just poured them with benzene and throw them into the pit.
Some people managed-- one of the friends, Rosenbaum was his name.
He told me, he was lucky.
He could roll somehow, and wasn't burned.
But he had his lesson.
So this Sonderkommando who had this shift at night for burning documents, brought me the photograph of my uncle in the camp.
And I was astonished.
I said, how did you get it.
And I knew-- How did he know?
Oh, so first of all, he knew it because he knew so well, he knew me so well, he recognized me, just by chance.
So he said, oh, that is Yehudah.
I will bring him the photograph from the camp.
Of course.
From these photograph, I knew only this uncle could have this photograph.
Because all other family didn't exist anymore.
So I knew that this uncle went there and perished.
At that time I thought also his wife-- but German ways of life and death, his wife, my Aunt [?
Rivka. ?] She survived.
Why?
Because she had to work and stay in Theresienstadt.
Because she worked in a special job, which was important for German military equipment.
Slate, you know, in that time they didn't have plastic.
They used slate for airplanes and heaven knows what.
And they worked for this slate.
And therefore she survived.
If that time, I thought, well both of them--
Yes.
Yes.
No, no.
No, no.
There was a couple of-- I don't know how many, but there were-- anyhow this is one of the stories of the Sonderkommando.
I saw the--
Well, they were very strange.
There's a special look in their eyes.
Not--
Very special people.
They got food as much as they wanted.
They got alcohol as much as they wanted.
They had in the time of evacuation of Jews from France, they could at my time do their cooking in the court yet, which was between our block and their block.
You wouldn't believe it.
They cooked on liquor, on spirit.
But the spirit was perfume from the transports.
They had so much of this.
And to us children, they brought sometimes even a bit of chocolate or some cigarette.
We could handle and change a cigarette for something.
And I can tell you another story.
I remember, this Kalman told me, this was not his only job.
He also had to help in other jobs, what they have done when a woman transport came, and there was a very beautiful woman, they left her to the very end.
And they took off their clothes.
I mean as if they gave here a kind of better treatment.
And sometimes he brought to the camp this underwear from the nicest Frenchmen to our camp.
Which we later on, as I told you, could--
Or from the other sources, from Canada.
Anyhow--
They were not anymore--
They were not anymore in the category of normal human beings.
They were something extraordinary.
Because they knew they would be killed.
No.
But they had prepared a kind of uprising.
I remember in the straw of Kalman's mattress, there was a-- something how you call-- a little--
A kind of little bayonet.
So he stole and had it prepared.
Unfortunately, it didn't help him much.
And I saw when I went to this crematorium number 2, also the place where Mengele made his vivisection.
This table from stone, with a special place where blood could drain.
And I saw, of course, an empty box of wood, where they were usually collecting.
It said go in peace, which were later melted and sent I think to the German [INAUDIBLE].
It was such a box about how much it could be, 60 on 40 and 40.
In the sauna, which was next to it, we also worked, of course, with our roll wagon.
And there I saw tremendous huge section with human hair, which were prepared to send off.
They also collected this hair, a tremendous lot of hair.
And of course, in the sauna, we little children could go in.
And there was staying sometimes a whole transport of 2,000 naked women, waiting at least.
I didn't know if they go to gas.
But they were in the sauna, which means the had a little chance maybe to go to work.
And that was one of our jobs.
Sometimes the clothes, sometimes from the crematorium we usually took the wood for normal, so-called normal use for heating.
But once we went after the uprising, I remember, because it was in crematorium number 3, I think.
We went and we loaded the ashes.
And we used it just for the-- you know-- for the ice.
Yeah.
Just that the walking should be more easily.
It was also one of our jobs.
OK.
[INAUDIBLE].
